---
title: "RL Math — Mathematics for Reinforcement Learning"
subtitle: "Shiyu Zhao's *Mathematical Foundations of Reinforcement Learning* (Springer 2025), lesson by lesson — derive every modern RL algorithm from the Bellman equation."
---

## Why this track exists

The seven synthesis tiers cover probability (T3, T5), linear algebra
(T5), gradient descent (T6), and a single-paragraph view of
reinforcement learning (T7.06). What the synthesis tiers *don't* do
is show how those ingredients combine to produce every algorithm in
modern RL — Q-learning, DQN, REINFORCE, A2C, DDPG — with the math
worked out in full.

This track is the **bridge**. It takes one carefully-chosen reference
(Shiyu Zhao's *Mathematical Foundations of Reinforcement Learning*,
Springer 2025) and walks lesson by lesson from "what is an MDP?" to
"deterministic policy gradient." Every algorithm is *derived* from
the Bellman equation. Every convergence proof is included. By the
end, the "RL algorithm zoo" feels like one optimisation recursion
specialised many ways — because that's what it is.

## What this track is for

Use this track if you want:

- **Derivations, not recipes.** Every algorithm is reached from
  first principles via the Bellman equation. You will know *why*
  Q-learning works and *why* DQN needs a target network — not just
  the pseudo-code.
- **Convergence proofs.** Robbins-Monro, Dvoretzky, Banach
  fixed-point — the real foundations of why iterative RL methods
  converge at all.
- **The unified view.** Sarsa, Q-learning, expected Sarsa,
  REINFORCE, A2C, DDPG — all are written in one pattern with
  one knob (the target). The algorithm zoo collapses to a tree.
- **Strict prerequisite discipline.** Each lesson depends on the
  previous one *and* declares its prerequisites in the synthesis
  tiers. Arrive without probability? You'll be sent to T3.13.
  Arrive without gradient descent? T6.10.

## Source

[**Shiyu Zhao** — *Mathematical Foundations of Reinforcement Learning*, Springer 2025](https://link.springer.com/book/9789819739431)

The author's [GitHub repo](https://github.com/MathFoundationRL/Book-Mathematical-Foundation-of-Reinforcement-Learning)
hosts free PDFs of the chapters, the lecture slides, and the grid-world
code used throughout.

::: {.callout-note}
**Citation, not transcription.** Per the same rule that governs the
Kiselev track, this track cites Zhao freely (chapter and section
numbers, theorem statements, structural choices) but does *not* paste
his prose. Examples are re-stated in our own words. The grid-world
example is used as a shared visual anchor — that idea is in the public
domain.
:::

## How a `mfrl` lesson is structured

The **same six-beat contract** as every other lesson in this repo
(*Touch* → *Wonder* → *Discover* → *Name* → *Apply* → *Connect*),
plus the four required callouts every lesson now carries:

- One `.fun-fact` (history / origin hook)
- One `.key-insight` (the intuitive aha)
- A `### Takeaways` block (3 bullets, intuitive)
- Inline glossary cross-links

Unlike CBSE and Kiselev, this track has no per-lesson tier-specific
addition (no "Board-Pattern Practice" or "Kiselev-style problems"
block). The shared visual anchor — the grid-world — appears in
*Touch* widgets across many lessons, not as a structural beat.

See [CLAUDE.md](https://github.com/rajeshpillai/applied-maths/blob/main/CLAUDE.md)
for the full authoring contract.

## Track structure

The track follows the book's two-part division:

### Part 1 — Basic Tools (lessons 1-22)

Foundations: MDPs, Bellman equations, contraction mapping, value/policy
iteration, Monte Carlo, stochastic approximation. These chapters earn
the right to *all later algorithms* — they are not optional.

| # | Topic | Chapter |
|---|---|---|
| 1-4 | MDP foundations: states, actions, transitions, policies, returns | Ch 1 |
| 5-8 | State values, Bellman equation, action values | Ch 2 |
| 9-12 | Optimal policies, BOE, contraction mapping, uniqueness of $v^*$ | Ch 3 |
| 13-15 | Value iteration, policy iteration, truncated PI | Ch 4 |
| 16-19 | Monte Carlo: mean estimation, MC Basic, exploring starts, ε-greedy | Ch 5 |
| 20-22 | Stochastic approximation: Robbins-Monro, Dvoretzky, SGD | Ch 6 |

### Part 2 — Algorithms (lessons 23-38)

Where the foundations cash out into the algorithms that drive modern
RL: TD learning, value-function approximation, policy gradient,
actor-critic.

| # | Topic | Chapter |
|---|---|---|
| 23-27 | TD learning: TD(0), Sarsa, n-step Sarsa, Q-learning, unified view | Ch 7 |
| 28-31 | Value function approximation: linear FA, semi-gradient TD, the deadly triad, Deep Q-Network | Ch 8 |
| 32-34 | Policy gradient: parameterisation, the policy gradient theorem, REINFORCE | Ch 9 |
| 35-38 | Actor-critic: QAC, A2C, off-policy AC, deterministic PG / DDPG | Ch 10 |

## Prerequisites from the synthesis curriculum

Every `mfrl` lesson declares its math prerequisites by linking back
into the T-tiers. The complete list:

| Prerequisite | What it gives us |
|---|---|
| [`t3.basic-probability`](../lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/13-basic-probability/index.qmd) | Sample space, events, $P(A)$ |
| [`t3.conditional-probability`](../lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/14-conditional-probability/index.qmd) | Conditioning, Bayes |
| [`t3.discrete-distributions`](../lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/15-discrete-distributions/index.qmd) | PMF, expected value |
| [`t3.sequences-series`](../lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/04-sequences-series/index.qmd) | Geometric series for the discounted return |
| [`t5.matrices`](../lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/01-matrices/index.qmd) | Matrix–vector form of the Bellman equation |
| [`t5.linear-systems-gaussian`](../lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/02-linear-systems-gaussian/index.qmd) | Closed-form policy evaluation |
| [`t5.partial-derivatives`](../lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/05-partial-derivatives/index.qmd) | Gradients of value / policy parameterisations |
| [`t5.continuous-distributions`](../lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/08-continuous-distributions/index.qmd) | Expectation as integral, PDFs |
| [`t5.variance-clt`](../lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/09-variance-clt/index.qmd) | Law of Large Numbers (justifies sample averages) |
| [`t5.sequences-series-convergence`](../lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/14-sequences-series-convergence/index.qmd) | Convergence of stochastic sequences |
| [`t5.real-analysis-foundation`](../lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/15-real-analysis-foundation/index.qmd) | Cauchy sequences, completeness for Banach FPT |
| [`t5.sets-logic-proofs`](../lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/11-sets-logic-proofs/index.qmd) | Proof discipline for the convergence proofs |
| [`t6.gradient-methods`](../lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/10-gradient-methods/index.qmd) | SGD variants, learning rate schedules |
| [`t7.topology`](../lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/02-topology/index.qmd) | Compactness for fixed-point existence |
| [`t7.rl-theory`](../lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/06-rl-theory/index.qmd) | The single-paragraph synthesis view (linked from every Connect) |

If any of these prerequisites is missing or under-sized for the new
track, that's a hint to add coverage in the synthesis tier rather
than duplicate inside `mfrl`.

## How to read this track

- **Read in order.** Each lesson depends on the previous one by
  design. Skipping forward will leave gaps that the prerequisite
  graph cannot patch.
- **Compare with `t7.rl-theory`** when you want the high-level
  picture — Tier 7's RL lesson is the single-paragraph version of
  this entire track.
- **Read alongside Zhao's PDF** (free at the GitHub repo). This
  track adds the *Touch* widget, the *Wonder* questions, the
  graduated hints, and the prerequisite scaffolding Zhao's
  graduate-textbook compactness leaves to the reader.

## Track status — complete (38 lessons)

Every chapter of Zhao 2025 is authored against the six-beat contract.
Each lesson has all required callouts (fun-fact, key-insight,
common-confusion, Takeaways with inline glossary cross-link,
Self-Assessment), graduated hints (Hint 1 / 2 / 3 / Full solution) on
every exercise, and a sister-synthesis link to
[`t7.rl-theory`](../lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/06-rl-theory/index.qmd).

The 58 RL-specific terms introduced across the track all have entries
in the site-wide [glossary](../glossary.qmd).

See the [tier index](../lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/index.qmd) for the
per-lesson list.
